# Enforcement Audit: `derived-claude-code-work.als`

Applies to the 4 Aligned assertions from `derived-claude-code-work-reconciliation.md`. For each Aligned assertion, this audit checks whether the rule is present **at the decision point** (the text a reader actually consults when deciding whether they may use the plugin), uses **gate language** (`must`, `requires`, `prohibited`) rather than advisory language (`consider`, `should ideally`), and is **complete at that gate** (a reader following only the decision-point text cannot miss it).

The rules in this model are **legal constraints, not software gates** — there is no runtime check that blocks a commercial user from invoking the skill. The "gate" is the text the user reads. Three candidate decision points exist in this repo, and each reaches a different audience:

| Decision point | Who reaches it | When |
|---------------|---------------|------|
| `README.md` § License | User reading the repo root (GitHub landing, clone inspection) | Before install, or during post-install review |
| `COMMERCIAL.md` | User who clicked the commercial-license pointer | When they already suspect they need one |
| `SKILL.md` (both skills) | User inside Claude Code as the skill activates | At the moment of use |

The third is the most load-bearing for A3 (commercial use requires license) because it is the only gate that fires **at the moment of commercial use**, not before or after. The first two require the user to already be reading the repo documentation.

## Enforcement verdict per assertion

| Assertion | Decision point(s) | Gate language? | Completeness at gate | Verdict |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|---------|
| A1 `UsersOwnNonDerivativeWorkIsFree` | `README.md` License, `COMMERCIAL.md` §"What your own code status looks like" | declarative grant ("**is yours**", "remain yours regardless of license tier") — appropriate for a grant, not a restriction | present and prominent in both surfaces | **Enforced** |
| A2 `DerivativesInheritCCForDefaultLicensee` | `COMMERCIAL.md` §"Who needs a commercial license" item 3; `LICENSE` §3(b) ShareAlike | gate: "Individuals shipping derivative works (forks, plugins that include this one) commercially" — phrased as who needs a license, implicit must | present in COMMERCIAL.md and LICENSE; **absent from SKILL.md and plugin manifests** | **Enforced (for careful readers)** — adequate because derivative shipping is not a moment-of-use action; the user must already be preparing a distribution when the rule applies |
| A3 `CommercialUseOfSkillRequiresCommercialLicense` | `README.md`:166 "**requires** a separate commercial license"; `COMMERCIAL.md`:7 "Anyone who uses this plugin as part of paid development work" | gate language in README ("requires"); gate language in COMMERCIAL.md ("anyone who") | **missing from `SKILL.md`** — the only gate that fires at the moment of use | **Mentioned but unenforced at the moment-of-use gate** (enforced at pre-install gates only) |
| A4 `PaidLicenseCoversEverything` | `COMMERCIAL.md` §"What a commercial license grants" (items "Permission to use…", "Permission to build…ship them commercially") | gate: "Permission to X" — explicit grant phrasing | present and complete in COMMERCIAL.md | **Enforced** (inside the commercial-license surface) |

## Summary

- **Enforced:** 3/4 — A1, A2, A4
- **Mentioned but unenforced (at the most load-bearing gate):** 1/4 — **A3 is not surfaced in `SKILL.md`**
- **Missing from gate entirely:** 0/4

## The enforcement gap — A3 in `SKILL.md`

**What the gap is.** The model proves, and all other source artifacts agree, that a user in a commercial context operating under the default CC license is not permitted to use the skill. The assertion holds at the legal layer. But a commercial user reaching the plugin via `/plugin install formal-methods@morozov-claude-plugin` may never read the repo root — they may install the plugin once and invoke the skill many times inside Claude Code. At each invocation, Claude Code surfaces `SKILL.md` (that is the plugin activation surface). If `SKILL.md` does not carry the disclaimer, the rule is not visible at the moment of use.

**Evidence of the gap.**

```
$ rg -i "commercial|license|NC|paid" skills/formal-modeling/SKILL.md skills/formal-debugger/SKILL.md
(no matches)
```

**Why the reconciliation report still marks A3 Aligned.** Alignment means "the source text agrees with what the model proves." `README.md` and `COMMERCIAL.md` agree with the model; they just aren't the surface the user reads at the moment of use. Enforcement is strictly stronger than alignment: a rule can be Aligned-but-unenforced when it's present in a source artifact but absent from the decision-point gate.

**Why it matters for this model specifically.** A3 is the *only* assertion whose violation happens silently (no derivative work created, no fork distributed, nothing shipped — just a skill invocation at a for-profit employer). The other assertions trigger on distribution events (A2 on shipping a derivative, A4 on invoking paid-license grants) where the user is already consulting documentation. A3 triggers on a normal `/` command inside an IDE.

**Self-reference:** `report.md` Recommendation #2 (lines 110–114) already specifies the fix:

> *Add a short disclaimer in `SKILL.md` near the top:*
> *"Using this skill as part of commercial development work requires a commercial license from the rights holder; see [link]."*
> *Claude Code will surface this when the skill activates, so users are aware at the moment of use.*

This is an unimplemented self-recommendation, not a new finding. The enforcement audit surfaces it as the load-bearing gap.

## Gate artifact check

For rules where the gate is a legal text (not executable code), the "gate artifact" is the text itself — there is no audit log or evidence chain. The gate either contains the rule in imperative language at the decision point or it doesn't.

| Gate condition | What the gate asks the reader | Recorded in | Field/clause | Imperative language? | Verdict |
|---------------|-------------------------------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|---------|
| A1 (non-derivative = free) | "Is my work a derivative?" → §1(a) test | `LICENSE` §1(a) | Adapted Material definition | yes — legal text is binding | OK |
| A2 (derivative → ShareAlike + commercial needs license) | "Am I shipping a derivative commercially?" | `LICENSE` §3(b), `COMMERCIAL.md` §"Who needs a commercial license" | ShareAlike clause; enumerated case list | yes — "must", "requires" | OK |
| A3 (commercial use itself needs license) | "Am I using the skill commercially?" | `README.md` License section, `COMMERCIAL.md` §"Who needs a commercial license" | "requires a separate commercial license" | yes at pre-install gates | **Missing at the moment-of-use gate (`SKILL.md`)** |
| A4 (paid license covers everything) | "Do I hold a paid license?" | `COMMERCIAL.md` §"What a commercial license grants" | Enumerated permission list | yes — "Permission to..." | OK |

One row fails the audit chain: A3 has no surface at `SKILL.md`, so the moment-of-use gate cannot fire.

## Recommended remediation

**Fix the SKILL.md gap.** Add to the top of both `SKILL.md` files (after the frontmatter, before the first body heading):

```markdown
> **License note.** This skill is free for noncommercial use under [CC BY-NC-SA 4.0](../../LICENSE).
> Using it as part of paid development work or to ship derivative works commercially requires
> a commercial license — see [`COMMERCIAL.md`](../../COMMERCIAL.md).
```

After the fix, re-run this enforcement audit and expect A3 to promote from "Mentioned but unenforced at the moment-of-use gate" to "Enforced."

**Low-priority optional fix.** Add a `license` field to `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` and `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` once (or if) the Claude Code plugin schema supports it. This helps automated tooling discover the license without parsing `README.md`, but does not change the legal-gate enforcement picture.

## Post-remediation re-verification

After applying the `SKILL.md` disclaimer, re-run:

1. `bash skills/formal-modeling/scripts/verify.sh system-models/derived-claude-code-work/derived-claude-code-work.als` — expect 4/4 pass (no model change needed; model is correct and does not encode the gate surface).
2. Re-run this enforcement audit manually — expect A3 to move to **Enforced**.
3. Update this file's header date and the summary row.

No re-reconciliation is needed because the reconciliation outcome does not change — only enforcement does.
